name: Omecihuatl
description: Omecihuatl, also known as 'Two Woman', is a primordial goddess in Aztec mythology, the consort of Ometecuhtli, and together they reside in Omeyocan, the highest level of heaven. She is a fundamental creator deity, embodying the feminine principle of duality and creation. From their union, all other gods and humanity were born. Omecihuatl is a powerful and enigmatic figure, representing the duality of existence, the balance of male and female energies, and the inherent creative force in the universe. While Ometecuhtli provides the masculine aspect of creation, Omecihuatl provides the feminine, nurturing, and generative force. She is often invoked in matters of birth, motherhood, and sustenance. Though not directly worshipped in temples like other Aztec deities, her presence is felt in the very fabric of existence. She is the ultimate mother, the source of all life and the embodiment of the divine feminine. Her influence permeates all aspects of Aztec cosmology, reminding everyone of the interconnectedness of all things and the importance of balance in the cosmos. She is the silent, powerful force behind creation, the yin to Ometecuhtli's yang, and a reminder that true power lies in the union of opposites. She is also sometimes associated with weaving and midwifery, further solidifying her role as a creator and nurturer. She is the essence of duality and the source of all possibilities.
subtitle: Lady of Duality and Creation

keyMyths.title: The Birth of the Gods
keyMyths.description: Omecihuatl and Ometecuhtli birthed the four Tezcatlipocas, who in turn created the world and the other gods. Their union is the foundation of the Aztec cosmos.

keyMyths.title: Residence in Omeyocan
keyMyths.description: Omecihuatl resides with Ometecuhtli in Omeyocan, the highest heaven, a place of duality and origins. This location symbolizes their elevated status and importance.

keyMyths.title: Source of Life
keyMyths.description: As the feminine principle of creation, Omecihuatl is the ultimate source of life and sustenance. Her influence is felt in all aspects of the natural world and human existence.

sources.author: Fray Bernardino de Sahagún
